summary:
The University of Bologna (founded 1088 AD) is the oldest university in continuous operation in the Western world, established in the northern Italian city of Bologna as a center for the study of Roman law — particularly Justinian's Corpus Juris Civilis, which had been rediscovered in a complete manuscript in the late 11th century. Bologna's law school attracted students from across Europe who sought mastery of the sophisticated Roman legal tradition that was being reapplied to the increasingly complex commercial and political life of medieval Italian cities, and its institutional model — the universitas, a self-governing guild of students who hired and paid their professors — became the template for European higher education.

Bologna's distinctiveness was its student-centered governance: unlike the later [[event:2267|University of Oxford]] model where masters controlled the institution, at Bologna it was the students who held contractual power over faculty, could fine professors for tardiness or unclear lectures, and organized the institution's affairs. This model reflected the Bologna student body's composition: mature adults, often already professionals, who had traveled long distances and paid for their education. The university's focus on law produced generations of trained jurists who staffed the growing bureaucracies of Italian city-states, the Holy Roman Empire, and the papacy, professionalizing administration and developing the legal frameworks of medieval European governance. Bologna's example inspired the establishment of universities across Europe — Paris (theology), Oxford, Cambridge, Padua, and eventually dozens more — creating the institutional infrastructure for the systematic transmission and development of learning that remains the foundation of modern higher education.
